Facts.text: St. Celestine I begins his reign as the Catholic Pope
35 bishops of Visigothic Gaul meet in the Council of Agde, shedding light on the moral conditions of the clergy and laity in southern France
Arnulf I the Old becomes landsheer of Flanders
Black Death Massacre: Jews who survived Black Death riots in Constance. Prince-Bishopric of Constance, Holy Roman Empire are burned to death; part of a wave of pogroms across Western Europe
Bishop Frederik of Bathe recaptures the Dutch city of Oldenzaal
Battle of Pinkie, Midlothian: English beat the Scots
Prussia signs trade agreement with US
British Honduras beats Spain in battle of St George
American naval commander Oliver Hazard Perry defeats the British in the Battle of Lake Erie
King Willem II opens Amsterdam Stock exchange
1st theater opens in Hawaii
George Mary Searle discovers the asteroid 55 Pandora
John Holden hits 1st recorded HR (Brooklyn vs NY)
-15] Battle at Cheat Mountain, Elkwater, West Virginia
Battle of Carnifex Ferry in Virginia, 170 casualties
Battle of Little Rock, Arkansas, begins when Union forces capture the city
Baptist minister supposedly invents rickshaw in Yokohama, Japan
Dutch Jurist Cooperation forms
1st international conference to promote anti-semitism meets Dresden Germany (Congress for Safeguarding of Non-Jewish Interests)
Congressman John R. Lynch presides over Republican National Convention
London taxi driver George Smith becomes the first person to be fined for drunk driving
Lattimer Massacre - a sheriff's posse kills twenty unarmed immigrant miners in Pennsylvania, United States
Empress Elizabeth of Austria is assassinated by anarchists Luigi Lucheni
2nd quake in 7 days (8.6) hits Yakutat Bay, Alaska
20.3 cm rainfall at Elk Point, South Dakota (state record)
Japanese battleship Mikasa explodes at her moorings after the magazine catches fire, killing 251 seamen
Great Idaho Fire destroys 3 million acres of timber
Cleveland Call & Post forms
George W. Buckner, named minister to Liberia
Lincoln Highway opens as 1st paved coast-to-coast highway across the United States
China becomes a member of the League of Nations
Cleveland Indians pitcher Ray Caldwell no-hits New York Yankees, 3-0 at the Polo Grounds, NYC
Treaty of St Germain: Austria ends incorporation with Germany
New York Yankees play their farewell home games in Polo Grounds, win both games of doubleheader against Philadelphia Athletics; move to Yankee Stadium the following season
Irish Free State joins League of Nations
Leopold and Loeb are found guilty of the murder of Bobby Franks in Chicago in "the crime of the century"
Allies-German treaty of Koblenz drawn
Germany joins League of Nations
Charles E. Mitchell, named minister to Liberia
Lord Cecil of British government says War was never so improbable
Dodgers' Johnny Frederick hits record 6th pinch-hit HR of the season
Independent City Owned Rapid Transit Railroad (IND) opens in NYC
1st Negro League All-Star Game, West beats East 11-7 (Comiskey Park)
2nd American Football League plays 1st game (LA 21, Pittsburgh 0)
Cleveland (Los Angeles) Rams plays their 1st NFL game, lose 28-0
Buckingham Palace hit by German bomb in London
British RAF drops 100,000 bombs on Dusseldorf, Germany
British troops land on Madagascar
British 8th army occupies Tarento, Italy
German troops occupy Rome and take took over the protection of Vatican City
Italian fleet anchors at Malta
Lieutenant-General Frederick Browning against Montgomery "But, sir, I think we might be going a bridge too far"
KLS-AM in Oakland, California changes call letters to KWBR (now KDIA)
Bijz Criminal division sentences war criminal Jacob Folks to life
British begin economic boycott of Iran
European Parliament forms in Strasbourg, France
Swanson sells its 1st "TV dinner"
WLUK TV channel 11 in Green Bay, WI (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
"Gunsmoke" premieres on CBS TV starring James Arness
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Maralinga, Australia
Public schools integrate in Louisville, Kentucky
WSYE (now WETM) TV channel 18 in Elmira-Corning, NY (NBC) 1st broadcast
WJCT TV channel 7 in Jacksonville, FL (PBS) begins broadcasting
Fencer Aladár Gerevich is part of the gold medal winning Hungarian sabre team at the Rome Olympics; Gerevich's 6th consecutive gold medal in the event
Yugoslavia dominates Denmark 3-1 to win the men's football gold medal at the Rome Olympics
American Ferrari driver Phil Hill wins Italian Grand Prix at Monza to clinch F1 World Drivers Championship; first American F1 world champion
Italian F1 Grand Prix, Monza: a crash causes the death of German driver Wolfgang von Trips and 15 spectators hit by his Ferrari
Mickey Mantlebecomes 7th to hit HR # 400
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
KLRN TV channel 9 in San Antonio, TX (PBS) begins broadcasting
WDCN TV channel 8 in Nashville, TN (PBS) begins broadcasting
20 black students enter public schools in Alabama
Phillies beat Houston Colt .45s, 16-0
Palestinian Liberation Army (PLA) forms
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Phillies 10,000th game to a decision since 1900, Phils beat Cards
WUND TV channel 2 in Columbia, NC (PBS) begins broadcasting
The Beatles' "Revolver," album goes #1 and stays #1 for 6 weeks in the UK
Chicago White Sox Joel Horlen no-hits Det Tigers, 6-0
Gibraltar votes 12,138 to 44 to remain British & not Spanish
Joel Horlen revives Chicago's pennant hopes with a 5-0 no-hit win
KVVU TV channel 5 in Henderson-Las Vegas, NV (IND) begins broadcasting
NY Mets sweep Montreal Expos putting them in 1st place for 1st time
US performs nuclear test at Grand Valley Colorado
KVUE TV channel 24 in Austin, Texas (ABC) begins broadcasting
3 British soldiers are killed in a land mine attack near Dungannon, County Tyrone
American freestyle wrestler Dan Gable wins the 68kg division gold medal at the Munich Olympics; only Olympic wrestler not to have a single point scored against him in the competition
American long-distance runner Frank Shorter achieves a famous win in the men's marathon with a time of 2:12:19.8 at the Munich Olympics
American men's 4 x 100m relay team of Larry Black, Robert Taylor, Gerald Tinker & Eddie Hart combine for a world record 38.19 to beat the Soviet Union for the gold medal at the Munich Olympics
An American double in the inaugural Olympic archery competition in Munich; John Williams wins the men's individual gold medal; Doreen Wilber takes the women's gold
Cuban boxer Teófilo Stevenson wins first of 3 consecutive Olympic heavyweight gold medals when Munich Games opponent Ion Alexe of Romania withdraws from the final because of a broken thumb
East v West in a women's 4 x 100m relay at the Munich Olympics; West Germany beats East Germany by 0.13 seconds for the gold medal
Finnish runner Lasse Virén wraps up the 5,000/10,000m double at the Munich Olympics; runs Olympic record 13:26.42 for the 5k gold medal
Kazimierz Deyna scores twice as Poland beats Hungary 2-1 in the Olympic football gold medal match; 80,000 at the Munich Olympic Stadium

West Germany beats Pakistan 1-0 to become first European nation since 1920 to win the field hockey Olympic title; marred by disgraceful behaviour of Pakistani players, fans & officials after losing the Munich final
WKAR TV channel 23 in East Lansing, MI (PBS) begins broadcasting
Yugoslavia beats Czechoslovakia, 21-16 in the inaugural Olympic handball final in Munich
Muhammad Alidefeats Ken Norton on a 12 round split decision for NABF heavyweight boxing title at the Forum, Inglewood, California
NASA astronauts aboard Skylab 3 prank Mission Control in Houston using recordings of Helen Garriott, wife of crew member Owen Garriott, to answer the morning call from earth
NFL New York Jets trade pro football's leading receiver Don Maynard to St Louis Cardinals
Controversial TV drama "Born Innocent" premieres on NBC TV, starringLinda Blairas an abused teen in a juvenile detention home
Portugal recognizes independence of Republic of Guinea-Bissau, their foremer West African colony
Teuvo Louhivouri sets cycling distance record of 515.8 mi in 24 hrs
American rock band Kiss release their 1st live album "Alive!"
2 airliners collide over Yugoslavia, kills all 176 aboard
5 Croatian terrorists capture TWA-plane at La Guardia Airport, NY
Blue Jays beat Yankees 19-3 with 20 hits
Christa Vahlensieck runs female world record marathon (2:34:47.5)
Hamida Djandoubi, convicted of torture and murder, is the last person to be executed by Guillotine in France
4th game of Boston Massacre; NY Yankees beat Red Sox, 7-4 to tie for AL East 1st place; Yanks out hit arch rivals, 67-21; score 42-9
Arlyne Rhode sets female footbow distance record (1,113 yds & 30")
3 Puerto Rican nationalists who attempted to kill Truman are freed
Expos Bill Gullickson, sets rookie record of striking out 18
Peter Comita replaces Tom Peterson of Cheap Trick
"Guernica" painting by Pablo Picasso returns to Spain

Decca releases Beatles audition on "Complete Silver Beatles" album, 20 years after label executives rejected them feeling that "guitar groups are on the way out" and "the Beatles have no future in show business"
Discovery returns to Kennedy Space Center via Altus AFB, Oklahoma
Sean O'Keefe (11) is youngest to cycle across US (24 days)
Te Maori exhibition opens at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York
U.S. 7th Circuit Court rules Soviet defector Walter Polovchak can't be forcibly returned to parents' country if it's deemed "not in the best interests" of underage defectors
Bryan O'Connor named chairman of Space Flight Safety Panel
-18] Hurricane Gilbert, kills 300 in Jamaica, Texas & Yucatan
Browns allow Pittsburgh only 53 net yards, a team defensive record
East Germans begin their flight to west (via Hungary and Czechoslovakia)
Ellis Island reopens as a museum
First time in NY Yankees history they are completely swept in a season series, as the Oakland A's beat them 12 games to 0
For the first time since 1968, all eight Grand Slam tennis champions are different people
Hard Rock Cafe opens in Las Vegas, Nevada
Iran agrees to resume diplomatic ties with Iraq
Mariner Matt Young becomes 21st AL'er to strike out 4 in 1 inning
Gail Devers runs US female record 100m hurdles (12.48 secs)
Lucy in Peanuts comics raises her Psychiatric Help from 5 cents to 47 cents
"The X-Files", created by Chris Carter and starring David Duchovny and Gillian Anderson, debuts on Fox
1,000th Boeing 747 jumbo jet is produced
Chong Hey swims female record 400m medley (4:01.67)/100m backstroke
47th Emmy Awards: NYPD Blue, ER, Frasier and Candice Bergen win
Cleveland's major league sports franchises, NFL Browns & MLB Indians, play simultaneous regular season games in Cleveland for only time, both win; Browns 22-6 v Tampa, Indians 5-3 v Orioles
Discovery buys the Travel Channel for $20 million
Indiana University fires basketball coach Bob Knight after an altercation with a student; Knight had spent 29 years at the school, compiling a 662-239 record
Switzerland, traditionally a neutral country, joins the United Nations
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN, described as the biggest scientific experiment in the history of mankind, is powered up in Geneva, Switzerland
10,000 miners demonstrate at Lonmin mines in Marikana, South Africa
16 people are killed by a suicide bombing in Kunduz, Afghanistan
29 people are killed by floods and heavy rain in Vietnam
A left over bazooka grenade in Cambodia explodes and kills three children
Teachers in Chicago strike effecting 350,000 students
16 people are killed after a series of bomb attacks across Iraq
Three people are killed after a Canadian Coast Guard helicopter crashes in the Arctic Ocean
Floods and landslides in North East Japan, centered on Joso force 90,000 to evacuate their homes
New human-like species - Homo Naledi announced by Scientists and a team of female archaeologists, found deep in caves in South Africa
Northern Ireland's First Minister Peter Robinson resigns in wake of IRA supposed killing of Kevin McGuigan
Artist Marta Minujin begins redistributing books from her art installation "Parthenon of Banned Books", honoring books burnt by Nazis, in Kassel Germany
California passes law to commit to carbon-free electricity sources by 2045

John Legend,Andrew Lloyd Webberand Tim Rice win Emmy Awards for television special "Jesus Christ Superstar - Live In Concert", making them all EGOTs (Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony winners); Legend is the first Black male artist to achieve EGOT status
South Carolina issues mandatory evacuation order affecting one million people ahead of Hurricane Florence
Bolivian wildfires have destroyed two million hectares (almost five million acres), of forest and grassland since August according to College of Biologists in La Paz
Iranian woman Sahar Khodayari dies after setting herself on fire during her trial, for entering a stadium disguised as a man in Tehran

Malaysia's National Disaster Management Agency delivers half a million face masks to Sarawak state, after more than 930,000 hectares (about 2.3 million acres) burnt in Indonesia cause hazardous levels of air pollution
US PresidentDonald Trumpfires his third national security adviser John Bolton
Austrian British researcher Martin Hairer wins Breakthrough prize for mathematics worth $3 million for work on stochastic analysis
California's August Complex wildfire becomes the largest recorded in state history at 471,000 acres (736 square miles)
Wildfires in Oregon cause 500,000 people to evacuate, 10% of the population with unprecedented 900,000 acres burnt

Rhino Records releases "Chicago Live At Carnegie Hall Complete', a 16-CD box set commemorating the historic April, 1971 series of sold-out shows at the famed venue by the American '"rock band with horns"

Best film at 79th Venice international film festival awarded to Laura Poitras’ documentary about photographer Nan Goldin 
 "All the Beauty and the Bloodshed"
U.N. Secretary-GeneralAntónio Guterresvisits and calls for global support for flood-ravaged Pakistan where 33 million people are displaced and damage is estimated at $30 billion
Collapse of two dams after heavy rain from Storm Daniel sends torrent of flooding through city of Derna, Libya, cutting it off completely, leaving over 4,000 dead and 8,000 missing
Luis Rubiales, head of Spain's football federation, resigns a month after grabbing and kissing Spanish player Jenni Hermoso, after the team's World Cup win
Philadelphia Phillies slugger Kyle Schwarber breaks MLB record hitting his 14th lead-off home run of the season in a 9-4 victory over Tampa Rays at Citizens Bank Park in Philadelphia
Pope holds one of the largest masses of his papacy with 600,000 people near Dili, capital of Timor-Leste
Rare survival, The Shem Tov Bible, combining Jewish, Christian, and Islamic artistic traditions, completed by Rabbi Shem Tov Ibn Gaon in Soria, Spain, in 1312, sells at auction for $6.9 million
English demandEdward VI(10) wed MaryQueen of Scots(5)
John Smithelected president of Jamestown colony council, Virginia
George Washingtonasks for a spy,Nathan Halevolunteers
Simón Bolívarnamed President of Peru
Hector Berlioz's first full-length opera "Benvenuto Cellini" premieres at the Salle Le Peletier in Paris
Famous American actorEdwin Boothmakes his stage debut aged 15 in Richard III in Boston
Karl Marxspeaks in Amsterdam
Pierre Savorgnan de Brazzasigns treaty with King Makoko of Congo
Lord Kitchenersails from Khartoum to Fashoda to confront French attempts to claim territory in the Sudan
NYC welcomes home GeneralJohn J. Pershingand 25,000 WWI soldiers
New York Giants rip Boston Braves, 22-1 at the Polo Grounds; future Baseball HOF infielderFrankie Frischgoes 6-for-6 before grounding out
Salvatore Maranzano is murdered by one of Charles Luciano's hitmen at the Helmsley Building in New York City
Canada, under the leadership ofMackenzie King, declares war on Germany
Lieutenant-GeneralOmar Bradleyarrives in Prestwick/London
Vidkun Quisling, head of the Nazi-controlled Norwegian government during WWII is sentenced to death for collaborating with the Nazis
Joe DiMaggiobecomes 1st to hit 3 HR in a game at Griffith Stadium
Attempting to handle New York Giants pitcherHoyt Wilhelm's knuckleball, catcher Ray Katt sets major league record with 4 passed balls
Abebe Bikilaof Ethiopia runs WR 2:15:16.2 barefoot to take out the men's marathon at the Rome Olympics; Africa's first ever Olympic gold medal
Future Hall of FamerJerry Lucasscores 23 points as the US wins its 5th straight men's basketball Olympic gold medal with a 90-63 drubbing of Brazil at the Rome Games
Gymnastics competition at the Rome Olympics concludes with the Soviet Union taking 10 of the 15 gold medals; Soviet gymnasts Boris Shakhlin &Larisa Latyninawin 3 gold medals each
NY YankeeMickey Mantlehits 643' HR over right field roof in Detroit
Stan Musialhits a HR in his 1st at bat as a grandfather
Rod Stewartrecords his 1st single "Good Morning Little Schoolgirl", a cover of an American blues standard first recorded by “Sonny Boy” Williamson in 1937
Muhammad AliTKOs Karl Mildenberger in 12 for heavyweight boxing title
Neil Diamondhas his first Billboard chart success with single "Cherry Cherry", eventually peaking at No. 6
MLB St Louis CardinalsLou Brockties (104) & then sets (105) baseball stolen base mark
Darryl Sittleris named captain of the Toronto Maple Leafs
The single point American Lotus driverMario Andrettiearns for finishing 6th at the Italian Grand Prix at Monza is enough to clinch his first Formula 1 World Drivers Championship
Larry HolmesTKOs Scott Frank in 5 for heavyweight boxing title
First episode of daily syndicated TV game show of "Jeopardy!" withAlex Trebekas host
Five days after hitting a HR for Yankees in a 12-2 win over the Mariners, MLB and NFL playerDeion Sandersreturns a punt 68 yards for a touchdown, his first
Indianapolis running backEric Dickersonrushes for 106 yards against San Francisco to become the fastest player to top the 10,000 yard plateau; 91st career game
Basilica of Our Lady of Peace consecrated by PopeJohn Paul IIin Yamoussoukro, Côte d'Ivoire, as the largest church in the world at 30,000 square metres (320,000 sq ft)
Rock bandNirvanarelease their single "Smells Like Teen Spirit", often dubbed the anthem of "Generation X"
US Senate Judiciary Committee begins hearings onClarence Thomas' nomination to the US Supreme Court
Mark McGwirejoinsBabe Ruthas only the players to hit 50 HRs in 2 consecutive years
Pedro Martínezpitches a near-perfect game against the New York Yankees, with only a solo home run by Chili Davis
52nd Emmy Awards:The West Wing, Will & Grace,James Gandolfini& Sela Ward win
Andrew Lloyd Webber's musical "Cats" closes on at Winter Garden Theater, NYC, after 7,485 performances, winning 7 Tony Awards, 3 Drama Desk Awards, and a Grammy Award
"There Will Be Blood", directed by Paul Thomas Anderson and starringDaniel Day-Lewis, premieres in New York City
Former Prime MinisterNawaz Sharifreturns to Pakistan after seven years in exile following a military coup in October 1999
Ai Moriwins gold in lead climbing at the IFSC Youth World Championships in Innsbruck, Austria
Australian newspaper the "Herald Sun" criticized for "racist" cartoon of American tennis starSerena Williams
UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres calls for urgent climate change conference saying "climate change moving faster than we are"
AlibabachairmanJack Masteps down, succeeded by Daniel Zhang
NovelistMargaret Atwoodpublishes "The Testaments", her follow-up to "The Handmaid's Tale"
Second US presidential debate between new candidate Vice PresidentKamala Harrisand former PresidentDonald Trumphosted by ABC in Philadelphia, with most commentators giving Harris the win
Elias Howetakes out a US patent for a lockstitch sewing machine
Mike the Headless Chicken is decapitated in Fruita, Colorado; he survives for another 18 months before choking to death.
Hurricane Irma makes landfall in the Florida Keys in the US as a category 3 hurricane
Alexios II Komnenos, Byzantine emperor (1180-83), born in Constantinople (d. 1183) [sources vary on exact day]
Alonso Pérez de Guzmán, 7th Duke of Medina Sidonia, Spanish Armada commander, born in Sanlúcar de Barrameda, Cádiz, Spain (d. 1615)
Hernando Arias de Saavedra, Spanish-American colonial governor, born in Asunción (d. 1634)
Ercole Porta, Italian church composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1630)
Nicholas Lanier, English composer, born in London (d. 1666)
Thomas Sydenham, English physician and author of "Observationes Medicae", born in Wynford Eagle, Dorset (d. 1689)
Romeyn de Hooghe, Dutch painter and goldsmith, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1708)
Henry Purcell, English organist and composer (Dido & Aeneas), born in Westminster, England (d. 1695)
Niccolò Jommelli, Italian composer, born in Aversa, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1774)
Carter Braxton, American farmer and signer (Declaration of Independence), born in King and Queen County, Virginia (d. 1797)
Bartolomeo Campagnoli, Italian violinist and composer, born in Cento, Papal States (d. 1827)
John Soane, English architect (Bank of England, Soane Museum), born in Goring-on-Thames, Oxfordshire, England (d. 1837)
Hannah Webster Foster, American author (The Coquette; or, The History of Eliza Wharton), born in Salisbury, Massachusetts (d. 1840)
Louis Alexandre Piccinni, Italian-French composer, born in Paris (d. 1850)
Nicolás Bravo, Mexican politician and soldier, born in Chilpancingo, Mexico (d. 1854)
William Mason, American politician, born in Lebanon, Connecticut (d. 1860)
John J. Crittenden, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Kentucky), born in Versailles, Kentucky (d. 1863)
Jacques Boucher de Crèvecœur de Perthes, French archaeologist (discovered flint tools in the gravels of the Somme valley), born in Rethel, Ardennes, France (d. 1868)
Marie Laveau, American Voodoo practitioner, born in French Quarter, New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1881)
Albert G. Blanchard, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Charlestown, Massachusetts (d. 1891)
Eleazer Arthur Paine, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Geauga County, Ohio (d. 1882)
Adolf von Doss, German sacred music composer and Jesuit priest, born in Pfarrkirchen, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1886)
Randall L. Gibson, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Versailles, Kentucky (d. 1892)
Joseph Wheeler, American Major General (Confederacy/Cavalry/Army of Tennessee), born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 1906)
Karl Merz, German-American composer (Brainard's Musical World), born in Bernsheim, Germany (d. 1890)
Charles Peirce, American scientist and philosopher known as "the father of pragmatism", born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1914)
Isaac Kauffman Funk, American publisher (Funk & Wagnalls), born in Clifton, Ohio (d. 1912)
Abel Hoadley, Australian confectioner (Violet Crumble), born in East Sussex, England (d. 1918)
Alice Brown Davis, first female Principal Chief of the Seminole Tribe of Oklahoma (1922-35), born in Park Hill, Indian Territory (d. 1935)
Anton Dreesmann, German-Dutch manufacturer (Vroom & Dreesman), born in Haselünne, Germany (d. 1934)
James Edward Keeler, American astronomer (rings of Saturn), born in La Salle, Illinois (d. 1900)
Marianne Von Werefkin, Russian-German-Swiss Expressionist painter, born in Tula, Russia (d. 1938)
Niels Hansen Jacobsen, Danish sculptor and ceramist (Trold, der vejrer kristenblod), born in Vejen, Denmark (d. 1941)
Jeppe Aakjær, Danish poet and novelist (Bondens Søn), born in Fly, Jutland (d. 1930)
Tor Aulin, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1914)
Caro Roma [Carrie Northey], American composer and singer, born in California (d. 1937)
Charles Collett, Chief Mechanical Engineer of the Great Western Railway (1922-41), born in London (d. 1952)
Ranjitsinhji Vibhaji, Indian cricket batsman (15 Tests, 989 runs @ 44.95, 2 x 100s) and politician (Maharaja of Nawanagar), born in Sadodar, Kathiawar, India (d. 1933)
Paul Scheinpflug, German composer, born in Loschwitz, Germany (d. 1937)
Georgia Douglas Johnson, African American poet and playwright (Autumn Love Cycle), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1966)
Paul Harvey, American actor (Spellbound, Calamity Jane, Heldorado, Jamboree), born in Sandwich, Illinois (d. 1955)
(Euphemia) "Phemia" Molkenboer, Dutch illustrator, ceramic artist, and furniture designer, born in Weesp, Netherlands (d. 1940)
(Theodora) "Dora" Pejačević, Croatian countess and composer (Symphony in F-sharp minor, Op. 41), born in Budapest, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1923)
Berthold Altaner, German Catholic church historian (Patrologie), and educator, born in St. Annaberg, Silesia (now Góra Świętej Anny, Poland (d. 1964)
Carl Van Doren, American literary critic and biographer ("Benjamin Franklin" - Pulitzer Prize, 1939), born in Hope, Illinois (d. 1950)
Johannes de Jong, Dutch clergyman (Roman Catholic as Archbishop of Utrecht, 1936-55), born in Nes, Ameland, Netherlands (d. 1955)
Hilda Doolittle, American poetess (Imagist movement), born in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania (d. 1961)
Giovanni Gronchi, Italian politician, President of Italy (1955-62), born in Pontedera, Italy (d. 1978)
Vilém Petrželka, Czech composer, born in Brno, Movravia (d. 1967)
Franz Werfel, Austrian-Bohemian novelist, playwright, and poet (Mirror Man, The Forty Days of Musa Dagh), born in Prague, Austria-Hungary (d. 1945)
Mortimer Wheeler, British archaeologist who introduced scientific methods, born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1976)
Arthur Compton, US physicist (1927 Nobel Prize for Physics for Compton Effect), born in Wooster, Ohio (d. 1962)
Al St. John, American vaudeville and silent film comic (Young and Dumb), and talkie character actor billed as "Fuzzy" St. John (Outlaws of the Plains), born in Santa Ana, California (d. 1963)
Maria de Jesus, oldest Portuguese supercentenarian (115 years, 114 days), born in Olival, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 2009)
George 'High Pockets' Kelly, American Baseball Hall of Fame first baseman (World Series 1921, 22; NL HR leader 1921; NY Giants), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1984)
Viswanatha Satyanarayana, Indian Telugu writer (Ramayana Kalpa Vrukshamu, Veyipadagalu), born in Nandamuru, Madras state, British India (d. 1976)
Adele Astaire [Lady Charles Cavendish], American dancer and entertainer, sister of Fred Astaire (Last Man on Earth; Millie), born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1981)
Robert Taschereau, Canadian judge and politician, Chief Justice of Canada, born in Quebec City (d. 1970)
Ye Ting, Chinese military leader, communist commander of the New Fourth Army, born in Guishan, China (d. 1946)
David L. Snell, American pianist and composer for films (MGM Studio), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1967)
Georges Bataille, French writer known for "Story of the Eye "and philosopher, born in Billom, France (d. 1962)
Hilde Hildebrand [Emma Minna Hildebrand], German actress (Port of Freedom), born in Hannover, Lower Saxony, Germany (d. 1976)
Nancy Lancaster, American decorator (Colefax and Fowler), born in Mirador, Virginia (d. 1994)
Bessie Love, American actress (Broadway Melody; Children of Damned), born in Midland, Texas (d. 1986)
Waldo Semon, American inventor (polyvinyl chloride), born in Demopolis, Alabama (d. 1999)
Toivo Pekkanen, Finnish writer (Wegwerkers), born in Kotka, Finland (d. 1957)
Max Shachtman, American Trotskyist and Socialist leader and writer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1972)
Lambertus "Bertus" van Lyre, Dutch composer (Hooglied -Song of Songs), conductor, and pedagogue, born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1972)
Alva R. Fitch, American lieutenant general in US army, Elvis' commanding officer , born in Amherst, Nebraska (d. 1989)
Raymond Scott [Harry Warnow], American composer ('The Toy Trumpet'), born in Brooklyn, New York  City (d. 1994)
Waldo Wedel, American Archaeologist (Great Plains prehistoric cultures), born in Newton, Kansas (d. 1996)
Mary Walter, Filipino actress (Shake, Rattle and Roll, Tatlo, Dalawa, Isa), born in Sorsogon, Philippine Islands (d. 1993)
William Oliver Everson, American poet, born in Sacramento, California (d. 1994)
Zephania Mothopeng, South African activist, President of the Pan-African Congress, born near Vrede, South Africa (d. 1990)
Robert Wise, American Academy Award-winning film director (The Day the Earth Stood Still; West Side Story; The Sound Of Music), born in Winchester, Indiana (d. 2005)
Redmond "Edmond" O'Brien, American actor (The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance, Sam Benedict, Johnny Midnight), born in New York City (d. 1985)
Franfo Fortini [Franco Lattes], Italian poet, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1994)
Miguel Serrano, Chilean author, diplomat and fascist (Esoteric Hitlerism), born in Santiago, Chile (d. 2009)
Rin Tin Tin, German shepherd dog who was a Hollywood star (Where the North Begins), born in Flirey, France (d. 1932)
Lex van Delden [Alexander Zwaap], Dutch composer (The Bird of Freedom), and writer (Canto della Guerra), born in Amsterdam, The Netherlands, (d. 1988)
Egbert van Paridon, Dutch actor and director (Flodder, The Assault), born in Amsterdam, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 2011)
Fabio Taglioni, Italian motorcycle engineer (Ducati), born in Lugo, Italy (d. 2001)
Alfredo Nobre da Costa, Portuguese politician (Prime Minister of Portugal, three months in 1978), and engineer, born in Lapa, Lisbon, Portugal (d. 1996)
Boyd K. Packer, LDS church apostle, born in Brigham City, Utah (d. 2015)
Hans Olof “Putte” Wickman, Swedish jazz clarinetist, born in Falun, Sweden (d. 2006)
Ted Kluszewski, American baseball 1st baseman (MLB All Star 1953-56; NL HR & RBI leader 1954; Cincinnati Reds), born in Argo, Illinois (d. 1988)
Roy James Brown, American soul singer ("Good Rockin' Tonight"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1981)
Beryl Cook, British painter, born in Egham, England (d. 2008)
Gwendoline "Gwen" Watford, British actress (Present Laughter, Cleopatra, Taste the Blood of Dracula), born in London, England (d. 1994)
Jean Vanier, Canadian philosopher and disabilities advocate, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 2019)
Leo P. Kelley, American sci-fi author (Dead Moon, Vacation in Space), born in Wilkes Barre, Pennsylvania (d. 2002)
Walter Martin, American Baptist minister and founder of the Christian Research Institute, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
Akio Yashiro, Japanese composer, born in Tokyo (d. 1976)
